text:
Mayor of Paris Anne Hidalgo swimming in the river Seine in Paris yesterday. The mayor took a dip in the river amid concerns over water cleanliness ahead of the Olympic Games, in which the river will host events. Photo: Getty

Over half a century after the Seine was declared biologically dead, Paris mayor Anne Hidalgo dipped into the river yesterday and declared it sufficiently clean for Olympic swimming competitions.

Under the gaze of a contingent of soldiers and police officers, and surrounded by an entourage of swimsuit-wearing city employees, Ms Hidalgo’s symbolic swim in the turquoise-brownish waters was broadcast live on national television. Hundreds of spectators also watched from the riverbanks and nearby bridges.

“It’s pure happiness,” Ms Hidalgo said after she climbed out, having fulfilled her pledge to swim in the Seine before the Olympic Games start in nine days.

Yesterday’s dip marked what French officials hope to be the end of a years-long nail-biter. Swimming in the Seine has been banned for a century because of health concerns, primarily due to wastewater that sometimes overflows from the city’s old sewerage system into the Seine. In 2015, Ms Hidalgo surprisingly announced a bold plan to make the river the star of the Olympic Games, with plans for a floating Opening Ceremonies and swimming competitions in the river.

But despite €1.37bn of investment, bacteria levels long remained above safe limits, leading to the cancellation of the swimming portion of Paralympic triathlon tests and of the Open Water Swimming World Cup last summer.

This year, the Surfrider Foundation environmental group reported “alarming” contamination, with testing at one site over six months showing higher-than-recommended levels of E coli and enterococci bacteria. The Paris mayor’s promised swim had to be repeatedly postponed, with explanations ranging from excessive rainfall to – most recently – France’s political turmoil.

As recently as last month, the ambitious project threatened to become an embarrassment. Officials were for the first time talking about a plan B for the highly anticipated Seine competitions.

But the gloom has given way to new enthusiasm in recent days, as the weather improved and the Seine’s water quality reached safe limits on most days this month, according to city measurements.

On Saturday, French sports minister Amelie Oudea-Castera became the first major French official to conquer the Seine. With president Emmanuel Macron’s government on the verge of resignation after this month’s election defeat, she rushed to the river. Her swim, which appeared designed to pre-empt Ms Hidalgo’s dip, did not go entirely according to plan – she slipped on the riverbank and then slid into the water. Mr Macron had also vowed to swim the Seine earlier this year but did not join.

Meanwhile, Ms Hidalgo – whose left-wing Socialist party is on the rise in the wake of elections – led a highly choreographed event. There were so many journalists that some had to follow the mayor’s dip from outside the security cordon.

“Now we’re ready for the games,” said Tony Estanguet, the president of the Paris 2024 organising committee, speaking after he accompanied Ms Hidalgo into the water. There is no guarantee that the Seine will still be as clean in two weeks, but weather in the coming days appears to be favourable.

Residents who followed yesterday’s swim said they are increasingly excited for the games.
title: Paris mayor Anne Hidalgo swims in the Seine, declares it clean for the Olympics | Irish Independent
description: Over half a century after the Seine was declared biologically dead, Paris mayor Anne Hidalgo dipped into the river yesterday and declared it sufficiently clean for Olympic swimming competitions.
keywords: Emmanuel Macron, Latest News Ticker, Paris 2024 Olympics, France
